summary: DeVault begins a multi-part series on intellectual property by first establishing a philosophical foundation for understanding property itself. He argues that property is a social convention, not a natural law, enforced ultimately through force, and justified primarily as a tool for managing scarcity. He then traces the logical progression from material property to immaterial property (like domain names) to intellectual property, noting that intellectual property fundamentally differs because ideas cannot be possessed or withheld by force. He concludes that intellectual property creates artificial scarcity to reward intellectual labor within a capitalist framework, teasing deeper critique in future installments.
key_points: Property is a social convention enforced by force, not a natural law — it could be replaced by different conventions with different trade-offs
Property rights derive legitimacy from managing scarcity, but can create artificial scarcity where none naturally exists (the idle screwdriver problem)
Intellectual property differs fundamentally from material property because ideas cannot be physically possessed or withheld — the relationship is historical, not possessive
Intellectual property creates artificial scarcity to reward labor in a system that depends on scarcity to function, avoiding deeper questions about capitalism and value
The labor theory of value provides a better justification for intellectual property than the subjective theory of value, which would likely produce a very different IP regime
money_quotes: It is important to note that the very possibility that one could illicitly assume possession of an object is a strong indication that "property" is a social convention, rather than a law of nature; one cannot defy the law of gravity in the same way as one can defy property.
We see the bounty of the natural world and impose upon it these imagined rights and privileges, divvy it up and hand it out and hoard it, and resort to cruelty if anyone steps out of line.
Our modern conception of property has the unintended consequence of creating scarcity where there is none by denying the use of idle screwdrivers where they are needed.
An idea cannot be withheld or seized by force. Instead, our relationship to intellectual property is defined by our history with respect to an idea.

A capitalist society fundamentally depends on scarcity to function, and so through intellectual property norms we create an artificial scarcity to reward (and incentivize) intellectual labor without questioning our fundamental assumptions about capitalism and value.
Intellectual property as a concept depends at least a little bit on Marx for its legitimacy, which I find very funny.
My willingness to accept property as a legitimate social convention is conditional on it not producing antisocial outcomes like homelessness or food insecurity.
key_insight: Intellectual property is a social invention that creates artificial scarcity for inherently non-scarce ideas, and understanding this requires first recognizing that all property is a social convention enforced by force rather than a law of nature.
